title: How to write a tag without it becoming a tag
checkLabels.t1: The opening bracket has a name
checkLabels.t3: The tag shows on the page
why.no-lt: You are still writing the angle bracket directly. The browser cannot tell "here is a tag" from "I am talking about a tag" — to it, the bracket means one thing: a tag starts here. Write it by name: <
why.no-gt: The opening bracket has a name, but the closing one does not. Both are needed: < and >
why.not-visible:
"

" still does not show as text on the page. Look at the result on the right — the sentence is mangled, the tag got executed instead of shown.
why.not-closed: A tag is open and never closed.
blocks.0.text: You decided to show off what you learned. You wrote it the normal way. Run it and see what comes out.
blocks.1.text: The sentence is mangled. The tag you were talking about is gone — instead of being shown, it got EXECUTED. The browser cannot tell "here is a tag" from "I am talking about a tag". To it, an angle bracket means one thing: a tag starts here.
blocks.2.text: The characters you write HTML with cannot simply be text inside HTML.
blocks.3.text: You write it by name, not by symbol
blocks.4.text: The fix is to write not the symbol itself, but its name. It starts with & and ends with a semicolon. The browser reads it, understands it, and finally draws the symbol — as text this time, not as a command.
blocks.5.items.2: & gives & — the ampersand itself, because it is special too
blocks.5.items.3: gives a space that will not break
blocks.9.text: Notice that & is written with an &? Here is the trap: since every name starts with &, the & itself can no longer be just a character. Otherwise the browser looks at "Price & delivery" and wonders where the name ends. So it has a name of its own.
blocks.11.text: is a plain space with one property: the browser is not allowed to break the line at it. Write 18 leva and the number will never be left alone at the end of a line with the unit on the next one. Useful with numbers, units and names.
blocks.12.text:
Resist the urge to line up several   to push something across. That is the same mistake as the row of
in the last lesson. Indentation is CSS.
blocks.13.text: And the tag for the code itself
blocks.14.text: Now the tag shows. But look at it — it sits there like an ordinary word in the middle of a sentence. Nothing hints that this is CODE and not chit-chat.
blocks.15.text: There is a tag for exactly this. It is inline, like <strong>, and it says: what is inside is code.
blocks.17.text: The browser shows it in a monospace font — the one where every letter takes up the same width. Exactly like the editor to your left.
blocks.18.text: Notice: <code> does not remove the need for <. The two go together. <code> says "this is code", while < makes sure the bracket shows at all. Skip the second and the browser will execute the tag again — this time inside the <code>.
blocks.19.text: And for a whole block of code
blocks.20.text: You want to show not one word but several lines — a whole example. Try it with <code>: the lines glue together. Of course. The browser swallows blank lines, you have known that for a while.
blocks.21.text: This is the one place in all of HTML where the browser HONOURS your spaces and blank lines. The tag is called <pre> — from "preformatted".
blocks.23.text: The two together: <pre> keeps the layout, <code> says it is code. You will always see them as a pair.
blocks.25.text: Careful with <pre>: it keeps EVERYTHING. Every space you add for tidiness in the code will show up on screen. So it is written tight, with no indentation — otherwise the indentation appears in the result.
blocks.26.text:
Your turn. Make the sentence actually say "I learned the

tag" — with the tag visible. If you like, wrap it in and fix the ampersand in the second paragraph too.
